claims.text: A 1989 geomagnetic solar storm knocked out power for 9 million people in Quebec for several hours.

evidence.quote:
In 1989, a solar storm hit Quebec.
The storm only lasted a few hours, but
it knocked out power for 9 million
people

claims.rationale: The March 13, 1989 Hydro-Quebec blackout is well-documented space weather history - caused by a geomagnetically induced current from a CME, lasted about 9 hours, affected roughly 6 million Quebec residents. '9 million' slightly overstates the count, and the outage spread beyond Quebec, but the core claim is accurate.

claims.text: Earth's magnetic field can flip in as little as a hundred years, and the last major flip occurred 780,000 years ago, making us overdue.

evidence.quote:
The last big flip happened 780,000 years
ago, and we're overdue for a big one

claims.rationale: The Brunhes-Matuyama reversal at ~780,000 years ago is established paleomagnetic fact. Reversals occurring in as little as a human lifetime is a recent (2019 Singer et al., 2020 China stalagmite) finding still under debate. 'Overdue' misstates the probabilistic nature - the intervals are highly irregular (tens of thousands to millions of years) and there is no true overdue threshold. Medium directTruth.

claims.text: Earth's magnetic field has weakened by about 10% since the 1800s, tracked by ESA's Swarm satellites.

evidence.quote:
The European Space Ay's Swarm
satellites track these changes

claims.rationale: Both figures are established geophysical fact. The dipole moment has declined about 9% over ~170 years of direct observations since Gauss's first measurements; ESA's Swarm constellation (launched 2013) provides high-precision ongoing monitoring. Host endorses in verdict section.

claims.text: The North magnetic pole is drifting toward Siberia at 34 miles per year and accelerating.

evidence.quote:
the North magnetic pole is moving 34
m a year towards Siberia

claims.rationale: The pole's accelerated drift from Canadian Arctic toward Siberia is well-documented. Rate has varied from ~10 km/year in the 1990s to ~55 km/year by late 2010s, then slowed slightly. '34 miles' (~55 km) sits at the high end of recent rates. Core trend is correct.

claims.text: The 1921 'New York Railroad' solar storm caused telegraph fires and melted railroad equipment worldwide.

evidence.quote:
In 1921, a big solar storm hit Earth.
Telegraph stations caught fire

claims.rationale: The May 1921 New York Railroad Storm is documented space weather. It did cause telegraph fires, disrupted signal systems, and aurorae visible to low latitudes. The claim holds up to historical record.

claims.text: The September 1859 Carrington Event was the most powerful recorded geomagnetic storm, electrocuting telegraph operators and producing aurorae near the equator.

evidence.quote:
September 1859 was worse.
The Carrington event

claims.rationale: The Carrington Event of Sept 1-2, 1859 is the canonical extreme-storm reference in space weather literature, with all described phenomena (telegraph shocks, Caribbean auroras, spontaneous paper fires) documented in primary sources.

claims.text: The US Department of Defense ConPlan 8888 is a real military document planning for a zombie apocalypse triggered by plasma collapse.

evidence.quote:
Conplan 8888, that's a real
military document, but it was a training
exercise

claims.rationale: CONPLAN 8888 is a real 2011 US Strategic Command training document, and the host himself acknowledges it in the verdict section as a staff-training exercise using fictional zombies as a low-stakes planning scenario - not an operational plan. Low-medium directTruth: document exists, but the framing as 'plans for the plasma apocalypse aftermath' misrepresents its purpose.

claims.text: Interdimensional 'phantoid' beings appear when the magnetic field weakens and plasma bleeds through, possessing recently-deceased organic matter.

evidence.quote:
The government calls them
phantoids, beings from another
dimension

claims.rationale: No scientific literature supports interdimensional plasma beings, plasma possession of corpses, or their emergence during geomagnetic events. The host himself explicitly denies this in the verdict section: 'no scientific proof for any of that.' Near-zero directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: [debunks] Host's verdict explicitly rejects phantoid claims as unscientific.

claims.text: A declassified 1976 CIA document titled 'Electromagnetic Effects on Human Tissue' shows plasma exhibits almost intelligent, consciousness-responsive behavior.

evidence.quote:
the event. A
declassified CIA document from 1976 is
titled Electromagnetic Effects on Hum

claims.rationale: A 1976 CIA document with roughly this title is in the released CREST archive, but it discusses radio-frequency bioeffects research (continuing Walter Reed Institute work), not consciousness-responsive plasma. The 'intelligent plasma' quote has no basis in the actual declassified text; it is a fringe-reading embellishment. Low directTruth.

claims.text: Soviet scientists since the 1950s studied 'fazoids' - plasma-ghost entities visible only during geomagnetic events.

evidence.quote:
50s. Their name for fazoids,
plasma ghosts. Soviet

claims.rationale: 'Fazoids' as a Soviet plasma-entity research program has no referent in peer-reviewed Russian plasma-physics literature or declassified post-Soviet archive release. The term circulates in fringe UFO/plasma-being websites without primary-source citation. Near-zero directTruth.

claims.text: Ancient Egyptian mummies contain deliberately-placed conductive metals (copper, gold, special salts) that formed circuitry pathways through the body.

evidence.quote:
Researchers at Cairo
University found that ancient Egyptian
mummies have high levels of conductive
metals

claims.rationale: Mummies do contain metals in specific inclusions - copper amulets, gold masks and foil, natron (salt) desiccant residue - but these are ritual/preservative, not circuitry. No peer-reviewed Cairo University study establishes electrical pathways. The circuit-body interpretation is not supported in Egyptology literature.

claims.text: Ancient monuments like Stonehenge, the Pyramids, and Nazca Lines form a global electrical circuit designed to channel or contain plasma during magnetic events.

evidence.quote:
The Nazcal lines in Peru. Massive
drawings on the ground

claims.rationale: A 'planetary circuit' of ancient megaliths has no support in archaeology, geophysics, or electrical engineering. Limestone and quartz are poor conductors relative to metals; the piezoelectric effect of quartz produces millivolts under pressure, nowhere near power-grid scale. Classic pseudoscience synthesis. Near-zero directTruth.

claims.text: There is no scientific proof for interdimensional beings, plasma zombies, or an ancient monument-plasma network.

evidence.quote:
There's no
direct proof connecting these festivals
specifically to plasma events or
magnetic field shifts

claims.rationale: Host's verdict-section debunk. The shorter-day-shorter-fire-is-sufficient explanation for solstice traditions, and absence of supporting scientific literature for phantoids/plasma-circuits, are both accepted by the host and by mainstream archaeology and space physics.

claims.text: A Carrington-class CME hitting a weakened modern magnetic field could knock out power grids for weeks or months, causing massive societal disruption.

evidence.quote:
ible. A big
coronal mass ejection or CME aimed at
Earth could knock out power grids for
weeks, ma

claims.rationale: NASA, NOAA, FEMA, and the UK Royal Academy of Engineering all publish risk assessments concluding a Carrington-class CME could cause multi-month blackouts in the US through transformer destruction. The worst-case-scenario risk is mainstream, not fringe.

claims.text: Marine Sergeant Jim Spark's classified 2009 exercise 'Broken Mirror' trained troops to contain biological entities emerging from a solar-storm-triggered anomaly.

evidence.quote:
Former
Marine Sergeant Jim Spark says he was
part of a classified exercise in 2009
cenamed Broken Mirror

claims.rationale: No DoD record, FOIA release, or journalism corroborates an Operation Broken Mirror or a Jim Spark account matching this description. The claim circulates in fringe podcasts without primary sourcing. Near-zero directTruth.
